title: Screening of the Newborn for Adrenoleukodystrophy

items.question: At what age would you have started daily doses of hydrocortisone for this patient?
items.explanation: Based on the algorithm provided in the text, daily doses of hydrocortisone should have been started at age 13 months (Answer D). The ACTH cutoff of 300 pg/mL or higher was chosen as it is reported that the adrenal cortex is maximally stimulated with an ACTH concentration of 300 pg/mL. However, in this case, the baby had evidence of hyperpigmentation and was not gaining weight well at age 7 months, which may have warranted starting daily doses earlier than the algorithm suggests. The decision to start hydrocortisone therapy should be based on a combination of clinical signs and symptoms, as well as laboratory testing.
items.reference: "On the basis of the algorithm (Figure), stress doses of hydrocortisone should have been started at 6 weeks of age (Answer A), when the peak cortisol concentration was 17.1 μg/dL (471.8 nmol/L). It is notable that the cutoff of 18 μg/dL (496.6 nmol/L) is based on older polyclonal assays and it is possible that newer monoclonal assays may have lower normal cutoffs."

items.question: Why is early detection of adrenal insufficiency through newborn screening important for boys with ALD?
items.answer: Because early monitoring, diagnosis, and treatment of adrenal insufficiency can prevent life-threatening adrenal crisis.
keyPoints: Early detection allows for timely initiation of treatment
Prevents severe complications such as adrenal crisis
items.reference: MAIN CONCLUSIONS: "ALD is the most common peroxisomal disorder and presents variably with adrenal insufficiency, neurologic disease, and testicular dysfunction. Recently, ALD was added to state newborn screening panels. Initial newborn screening data suggest that ALD may be more prevalent than previously described and adrenal insufficiency may develop in some affected boys during early infancy. Early monitoring, diagnosis, and treatment of adrenal insufficiency can prevent life-threatening adrenal crisis. The Pediatric Endocrine Society developed an algorithm to guide clinicians caring for boys with ALD identified by newborn screening. The publication suggests a monitoring schedule for adrenal insufficiency, addresses the ambiguities in cortisol and ACTH test results, and guides therapeutic decisions for boys with ALD."

items.question: How should clinicians monitor boys identified by newborn screening for adrenal insufficiency due to Adrenoleukodystrophy (ALD)?
items.answer: The Pediatric Endocrine Society developed an algorithm that guides clinicians in monitoring boys with ALD identified by newborn screening. This algorithm suggests a specific schedule for monitoring adrenal insufficiency, addresses ambiguities in cortisol and ACTH test results, and provides therapeutic decisions for these patients.
keyPoints: An algorithm from the Pediatric Endocrine Society is used to guide monitoring of boys with ALD identified by newborn screening.
The algorithm includes a specific schedule for monitoring adrenal insufficiency.
items.reference: MAIN CONCLUSIONS: "The Pediatric Endocrine Society developed an algorithm to guide clinicians caring for boys with ALD identified by newborn screening. The publication suggests a monitoring schedule for adrenal insufficiency, addresses the ambiguities in cortisol and ACTH test results, and guides therapeutic decisions for boys with ALD."
